name: Zen · Expert
description: Deep cybersecurity, cryptography and cloud/network security. For Guido. Not easy.

questions.text: Why is AES-GCM generally preferred over AES-CBC for new designs?
options: It runs faster but offers no integrity checking at all
It uses a much shorter key than CBC does
It authenticates as well as encrypts, in one step
It needs no initialisation vector at all
questions.explanation: GCM is an AEAD mode: it detects tampering via an auth tag, whereas CBC gives no integrity and is prone to padding-oracle attacks.

questions.text: A nonce in AES-GCM must never be reused with the same key because…
options: It makes each encryption noticeably slower
It can expose plaintext and let the tag be forged
It gradually corrupts the underlying key
GCM does not use a nonce in the first place, so nothing happens
questions.explanation: Catastrophic for GCM: repeating (key, nonce) allows forgery of the authentication tag and recovery of plaintext relationships.

questions.text: In TLS 1.3, forward secrecy is guaranteed because…
options: Static RSA key exchange is now mandatory
Ephemeral (EC)DHE keys protect past sessions
Sessions are never written to any log
Certificates are rotated automatically hourly
questions.explanation: TLS 1.3 removed static RSA key transport; ephemeral Diffie-Hellman means compromising the server key later can't decrypt captured past traffic.

questions.text: What does mutual TLS (mTLS) add over standard TLS?
options: It uses far larger keys than standard TLS
It makes the TLS handshake noticeably faster
The server verifies the client's certificate too
It removes the need for a certificate authority
questions.explanation: mTLS authenticates the client to the server as well, common for service-to-service auth in zero-trust architectures.

questions.text: Why store passwords with bcrypt/scrypt/Argon2 rather than SHA-256?
options: They are simply newer than SHA-256
They are reversible, so passwords can be recovered
They are deliberately slow and salted per user
They need no salt, being secure already
questions.explanation: Fast hashes like SHA-256 are trivially brute-forced; adaptive password hashes add work factor and per-user salt to slow attackers.

questions.text: In OAuth 2.0 / OIDC, why must a public client (SPA/mobile) use PKCE?
options: It makes the login flow noticeably faster
It binds the code to the client, blocking interception
It lets the client avoid using HTTPS
It bypasses the token endpoint entirely during the exchange
questions.explanation: PKCE's code_verifier/challenge prevents a stolen authorization code from being redeemed by an attacker, essential without a client secret.

questions.text: A JWT signed with 'alg: none' is dangerous because…
options: The token expires the instant it is issued
The token becomes far too large to transmit over the wire
If accepted, tokens can be forged with no signature
The token can no longer carry any claims
questions.explanation: The classic JWT vuln: verifiers must pin expected algorithms and reject 'none' (and not confuse HS256/RS256 key handling).

questions.text: The core purpose of threat modeling (e.g. STRIDE) is to…
options: Patch known vulnerabilities more quickly after release
Replace penetration testing entirely
Encrypt data at rest and in transit
Identify and prioritise threats before building
questions.explanation: STRIDE (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Info disclosure, DoS, Elevation) structures reasoning about what can go wrong per component.

questions.text: In a zero-trust model, network location…
options: Grants automatic trust inside the perimeter
Fully replaces the need for a user identity
Grants no implicit trust; every request is verified
Has no bearing on the access policy at all
questions.explanation: Zero trust drops the 'trusted internal network' assumption; identity, device posture and policy gate every access.

questions.text: Why is a strict Content-Security-Policy an effective XSS mitigation?
options: It signs the session cookies against tampering
It encrypts the whole page before sending it
It hides the DOM from any injected script
It restricts which script sources may execute
questions.explanation: A well-scoped CSP (no unsafe-inline, allow-listed origins) stops most injected scripts from running even if injection occurs.

questions.text: SSRF is especially dangerous in cloud because an attacker can…
options: It can hit the metadata service to steal credentials
It can overwrite the instance's CPU microcode
It can delete the whole account's DNS records
It can silently bypass TLS on every request
questions.explanation: Server-side request forgery can hit link-local metadata endpoints; IMDSv2's session tokens exist precisely to mitigate this.

questions.text: The principle of defence in depth means…
options: Trusting anything already inside the perimeter
One very strong firewall is entirely sufficient
Encrypting the same data two separate times
Layered controls so no single failure is fatal
questions.explanation: Multiple overlapping controls (network, host, app, identity, data) ensure a single bypass doesn't hand over everything.

questions.text: Certificate pinning protects against…
options: Certificates that have simply expired
A rogue or mis-issued CA presenting a valid cert
Users choosing weak or reused passwords
A flood of malicious traffic overwhelming the server
questions.explanation: Pinning ties a client to specific keys/certs so a rogue but technically-valid certificate is still rejected — at the cost of rotation complexity.

questions.text: Why segment an OT/ICS network from IT (as in a Purdue model)?
options: Purely to lower the overall network cost
To make the industrial controllers run noticeably faster
To reduce the amount of cabling required
To isolate safety-critical control systems from IT
questions.explanation: Segmentation limits lateral movement so an IT breach can't directly reach industrial controllers — critical in manufacturing.

questions.text: In envelope encryption (e.g. with a KMS), the data key is…
options: Per-object, itself wrapped by a master key in the KMS
Stored in plaintext directly next to the encrypted data
Identical to the master key for every object
Sent to the client unencrypted on each request
questions.explanation: You encrypt data with a local data key, then store that data key wrapped by the KMS master key — enabling scale and key rotation.

questions.text: A padding oracle attack exploits…
options: Users selecting weak, easily guessed passwords
Reuse of the same nonce across GCM messages
Certificates that have passed their expiry date
A system revealing whether decrypted padding is valid
questions.explanation: Distinguishable padding-error responses on CBC let an attacker decrypt/forge without the key — mitigated by authenticated encryption.

questions.text: Why prefer short-lived, automatically-rotated credentials over long-lived keys?
options: They shrink the exposure window if ever leaked
They are cheaper to store than long-lived keys
They make each API call noticeably faster
They remove the need for any access policy
questions.explanation: Ephemeral credentials (e.g. STS/role assumption, workload identity) mean a leaked secret is useless quickly.

questions.text: A confused-deputy problem in cloud IAM occurs when…
options: A privileged service is tricked into acting for an attacker
A single user simply forgets their own password
Two different users end up sharing one role
Multi-factor authentication has been switched off account-wide
questions.explanation: A trusted intermediary misuses its authority for a caller; scoping conditions (like AWS ExternalId) prevent cross-account abuse.

questions.text: DNSSEC provides…
options: Full encryption of every DNS query sent
Authentication and integrity of DNS records
Noticeably faster domain-name lookups
Blocking of advertising and tracking domains
questions.explanation: DNSSEC signs records so resolvers can detect forged answers; note it provides integrity, not confidentiality (that's DoH/DoT).

questions.text: In an incident, why capture volatile memory before powering off?
options: Live memory holds keys and state lost on shutdown
It is simply faster than imaging the disk
Powering off first resets the system logs
Disk contents vanish forever the moment power is cut
questions.explanation: Order-of-volatility: RAM, network state and caches vanish on power-off, so acquire them first to preserve evidence.
